core_meaning: よう has two major roles you'll meet constantly: (1) as a noun／na-adjective meaning 'way, manner, appearance, likeness' (e.g. <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>のような<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby> = 'a person like him'); (2) as a grammatical ending ～ように／～ような expressing similarity, manner, or a goal/wish. It is not one fixed word but a highly flexible function word that glues descriptions together.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>水<rt>みず</rt></ruby>を<ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>むように、すらすらと<ruby>英語<rt>えいご</rt></ruby>を<ruby>話<rt>はな</rt></ruby>す。
sentences.translation: She speaks English fluently, like she's drinking water.
sentences.notes: ように here draws a vivid simile — 'in the manner of doing X' — one of its core uses.

sentences.jp: <ruby>早<rt>はや</rt></ruby>く<ruby>治<rt>なお</rt></ruby>るように、<ruby>毎日<rt>まいにち</rt></ruby>ちゃんと<ruby>薬<rt>くすり</rt></ruby><ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>んでね。
sentences.translation: Take your medicine properly every day so you get better quickly.
sentences.notes: ～ように before a request or instruction expresses a goal or desired outcome ('so that / in order that').

sentences.jp: <ruby>先生<rt>せんせい</rt></ruby>に<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>われたように、レポートを<ruby>書<rt>か</rt></ruby>き<ruby>直<rt>なお</rt></ruby>しました。
sentences.translation: I rewrote the report as my teacher told me to.
sentences.notes: <ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>われたように = 'as (someone) said' — ように attached to a plain past verb shows conformity to a model or instruction.

sentences.jp: なんか、<ruby>夢<rt>ゆめ</rt></ruby>みたいな<ruby>話<rt>はなし</rt></ruby>だよね。
sentences.translation: It sounds like something out of a dream, doesn't it?
sentences.notes: みたいな is the casual spoken equivalent of ような; both express 'like / resembling', but ような is slightly more formal.

sentences.jp: <ruby>皆様<rt>みなさま</rt></ruby>にご<ruby>理解<rt>りかい</rt></ruby>いただけますように、わかりやすい<ruby>資料<rt>しりょう</rt></ruby>を<ruby>用意<rt>ようい</rt></ruby>いたしました。
sentences.translation: We have prepared easy-to-understand materials so that everyone may understand.
sentences.notes: In formal/written contexts ～ますように expresses a strong wish or intention directed toward a result.

dont_use: Don't swap ような and ように carelessly: ような modifies nouns (<ruby>夢<rt>ゆめ</rt></ruby>のような<ruby>話<rt>はなし</rt></ruby>), while ように modifies verbs or ends a clause (<ruby>忘<rt>わす</rt></ruby>れないように<ruby>書<rt>か</rt></ruby>いた). Also, don't use ように to mean 'it seems' in the sense of the speaker's current judgment — that role belongs to ようだ／ようです as a standalone predicate. Finally, don't confuse the goal-ように (<ruby>上手<rt>じょうず</rt></ruby>になるように<ruby>練習<rt>れんしゅう</rt></ruby>する) with the manner-ように (<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>われたようにやる); context and verb type determine which reading applies.

confused_with.contrast: Learners often reach for ような when speaking casually, but native speakers almost always say みたいな／みたいに in conversation (e.g. ×<ruby>夢<rt>ゆめ</rt></ruby>のような→ ○<ruby>夢<rt>ゆめ</rt></ruby>みたいな in casual speech). ような sounds bookish or stiff in everyday chat among friends. Use ような in writing, formal speech, or set expressions; use みたいな／みたいに when you're just talking with people.

confusion_set.use_when: Modifying a noun with a comparison or resemblance in standard/formal register.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>氷<rt>こおり</rt></ruby>のような<ruby>手<rt>て</rt></ruby>だね — 'Your hands are like ice.'

confusion_set.use_when: Modifying a verb/adjective to express manner ('as/like') or a goal ('so that').
confusion_set.example: <ruby>聞<rt>き</rt></ruby>こえるように、もっと<ruby>大<rt>おお</rt></ruby>きな<ruby>声<rt>こえ</rt></ruby>で<ruby>話<rt>はな</rt></ruby>してください — 'Please speak louder so I can hear you.'

confusion_set.use_when: Casual spoken equivalent of ような／ようだ — similarity or hearsay in everyday conversation.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>、<ruby>風邪<rt>かぜ</rt></ruby>みたいだよ — 'Looks like he's got a cold.'

frequency: Extremely common across all registers. ように／ような appear constantly in both speech and writing. The formal written ～ますように is especially frequent in business email sign-offs and formal requests. Even beginners will encounter this within their first few months.
anki_hint: Think of ように as 'in the way of / so that' (verb modifier) and ような as 'like / resembling' (noun modifier) — the な vs. に ending tells you what it's connecting to, just like other na-adjectives (<ruby>静<rt>しず</rt></ruby>かな vs. <ruby>静<rt>しず</rt></ruby>かに).
